# Abstract

**Background:** Most of what we know about research data sharing comes from
self-reported surveys and post-hoc reflections. The actual operational processes through
which data sharing is coordinated across institutions have rarely been observed directly.
This gap is particularly consequential as Canada's Tri-Agency Research Data Management
Policy places growing data sharing obligations on institutions, based on limited empirical
evidence about what that coordination work actually involves.

**Methods:** We analyzed 6,833 emails from the inbox of a central operations manager
at the Canadian COVID-19 Immunity Task Force (CITF) Databank, spanning
correspondence with 100 partnering studies from February 2021 to September 2025.
Emails were preprocessed using rule-based cleaning and automated de-identification,
with French text translated into English using neural machine translation. We applied
Non-negative Matrix Factorization (NMF) for topic modeling, selecting k=14 topics based
on coherence (c_v = 0.768), granularity, and topic diversity. Topic labels and thematic
groupings were generated using a locally deployed large language model
(Meta-Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct) and refined by the research team. Topics were analyzed
across six phases of the data sharing workflow and five sender categories.

**Results:** NMF identified 14 topics organized into four thematic categories: Agreements
and Contracts, Data Operations, Study Documentation, and Coordination. Three principal
findings emerged. First, governance and technical operations ran largely in parallel, with
Document Review and Comments serving as the primary point of convergence between
legal and technical workstreams. Second, data governance concerns persisted throughout
all phases of the data sharing lifecycle rather than being resolved early; even in the final
publication phase, legal and administrative topics accounted for 17% of emails. Third,
coordination labour was highly concentrated among Databank personnel (up to 115
emails per sender) relative to study teams (5--7 emails per sender), with women
disproportionately occupying high-volume coordination roles in the earliest and most
labour-intensive phases.

**Conclusions:** The 18:1 ratio of administrative to technical time — with studies
spending a median of 395 days in data sharing agreement negotiation compared to 22
days for technical cataloguing — reveals that data sharing is primarily an administrative
and governance challenge rather than a technical one. These findings carry direct
implications for how Canadian research data infrastructure is resourced, governed, and
staffed, particularly as institutions build capacity to meet growing data sharing
obligations under national policy frameworks.

**Keywords:** research data sharing; data governance; topic modeling; health data
infrastructure; coordination labour; COVID-19; Canada
